title: 發生車禍後半年內撞咗幾次嘢，應該點做？

replies.msg:
利申本身係麻瓜
上年年中坐巴士車頭撞車，傷咗七十幾個人但我奇蹟輕傷最多比車頭啲玻璃碎𠝹傷同撞到膝頭
跟住就開始發生啲奇怪又解釋唔到嘅事
我自己本身有信仰唔係無神論者
但我唔想所有嘢都扯去怪力亂神度解釋
我一直都無親眼見過但發生車禍後就見到
唔知關唔關事
同埋見到嗰陣唔算好驚嘅，但我唔知事件演化落去會點
問咗個法科朋友，佢話如果佢地企定定喺度望實我先搵佢
咁我而家唔知係咪可以無視住先


第一單

當時夜晚返到屋企樓下，大堂有四部𨋢，左手兩部𨋢係上較高嘅樓層，右手兩部就係上較低嘅樓層
咁我就住低樓層嘅，我大堂出面係玻璃門，我當時喺玻璃門外撳緊密碼，一路撳嗰陣下意識隔住玻璃門望下啲𨋢停喺幾樓，本身兩架𨋢都低喺地下，跟住見到有個橙色衫嘅女人入咗低樓層左手邊嘅𨋢，然後閂咗門
我心諗：好彩仲有另一部停喺地下
然後我一撳𨋢嗰陣，個橙衫女人入嗰部𨋢開咗，但入面無人，跟住我毛管戙，但都照入𨋢上樓扮無嘢


第二單
大概夜晚8點幾，我當時一路望電話一路等過馬路，我下意識望一望前面條大直路有咩人，見到有個男仔一路玩電話一路行，然後有個長頭髮女仔低住頭咁跟喺佢後面行
當時我就諗：哦，一男一女
跟住就繼續低頭撳電話，到過馬路嗰陣，個男仔經過我身邊，得返個男仔，個女仔唔見咗，但條大直路無任何分岔路
然後我就四圍望個女仔究竟去咗邊，不過見唔到，又廢時問個男仔嚇親人
呢次我就唔驚嘅因為唔係跟住我


第三單
有次我行人天橋，成條天橋得我一個人行，行到上橋頭，望落橋尾，見到有套男裝衫喺度行路，但入面係無人，跟住就消失咗，我即刻追上去睇，但乜都睇唔到，然後再去檢查多次有無物件令我睇錯，都係無發現


第四單
我當時喺屋企廁所，然後無插掣嘅洗衣機無啦啦自己震，震到我擺喺洗衣機上面嘅衫喺度郁，震咗下就停咗了（以前從來無發生過呢啲事）


大家點睇呀？
留名

睇得多黑市
巴打，去睇下精神科好喎

大概夜晚8點幾，我當時一路望電話一路等過馬路，我下意識望一望前面條大直路有咩人，見到有個男仔一路玩電話一路行，然後有個長頭髮女仔低住頭咁跟喺佢後面行

即使20年前的事故可以要求賠償損失

Page v Smith [1995] 2 All ER 736

The plaintiff was involved in a collision with the defendant when the latter failed to give way when turning out of a side road. The plaintiff was physically unhurt in the collision, but the accident caused him to suffer the onset of myalgic encephalomyelitis (ME) from which he had suffered for about 20 years but which was then in remission. The recrudescence of ME was likely to prevent him from ever working again. The plaintiff brought an action against the defendant claiming damages for chronic and permanent ME. The defendant admitted liability for the accident but disputed liability for damages. The judge awarded the plaintiff damages of £162,153 on the ground that once it was established that the plaintiff had ME, that a relapse or recrudescence of his condition could be triggered by the trauma of an accident of moderate severity and that he had suffered nervous shock as the result of being involved in the accident, the aggravation of his condition was a foreseeable consequence for which the defendant was liable. The defendant appealed, contending (i) that the plaintiff had not proved a causal connection between the accident and the aggravation of his condition and (ii) that the judge, in deciding that the plaintiff's injury was foreseeable, had failed to consider whether a person of reasonable fortitude would have suffered shock from the accident and had wrongly decided that foreseeability of injury from nervous shock was not necessary in the case of a plaintiff who had been directly involved in the accident rather than a mere spectator. The Court of Appeal allowed the appeal on the grounds, inter alia, that in claims for damages due to nervous shock it was in all cases incumbent on the plaintiff to prove that injury by nervous shock was reasonably foreseeable by the defendant. The plaintiff appealed to the House of Lords.


Held – (Lord Keith and Lord Jauncey dissenting) Applying the principle that the defendant had to take his victim as he found him, a negligent driver sued for damages arising out of a motor vehicle accident caused by him was liable for damages for nervous shock suffered by a primary victim of the accident if personal injury of some kind to that person was reasonably foreseeable as a result of the accident. In the case of primary victims of an accident the test in all cases was the same, namely whether the defendant could reasonably foresee that his conduct would expose the plaintiff to the risk of personal injury, whether physical or psychiatric. In the case of an affirmative answer, the duty of care was established, even though physical injury did not in fact occur. The plaintiff was not required to prove that injury by nervous shock was reasonably foreseeable by the defendant and it was irrelevant that the defendant could not have foreseen that the plaintiff had an 'eggshell personality' since (per Lord Browne-Wilkinson) it was established by medical science that psychiatric illness could be suffered as a consequence of an accident although not demonstrably attributable directly to physical injury to the plaintiff. It followed that the appeal would be allowed

創傷後壓力綜合症（Post-traumatic stress disorder，簡稱PTSD，又稱創傷後遺症）是指人在經歷過情感、戰爭、交通事故等創傷事件後產生的精神疾病[1]。其症狀包括會出現不愉快的想法、感受或夢，接觸相關事物時會有精神或身體上的不適和緊張

即使20年前的事故可以要求賠償損失

Page v Smith [1995] 2 All ER 736

The plaintiff was involved in a collision with the defendant when the latter failed to give way when turning out of a side road. The plaintiff was physically unhurt in the collision, but the accident caused him to suffer the onset of myalgic encephalomyelitis (ME) from which he had suffered for about 20 years but which was then in remission. The recrudescence of ME was likely to prevent him from ever working again. The plaintiff brought an action against the defendant claiming damages for chronic and permanent ME. The defendant admitted liability for the accident but disputed liability for damages. The judge awarded the plaintiff damages of £162,153 on the ground that once it was established that the plaintiff had ME, that a relapse or recrudescence of his condition could be triggered by the trauma of an accident of moderate severity and that he had suffered nervous shock as the result of being involved in the accident, the aggravation of his condition was a foreseeable consequence for which the defendant was liable. The defendant appealed, contending (i) that the plaintiff had not proved a causal connection between the accident and the aggravation of his condition and (ii) that the judge, in deciding that the plaintiff's injury was foreseeable, had failed to consider whether a person of reasonable fortitude would have suffered shock from the accident and had wrongly decided that foreseeability of injury from nervous shock was not necessary in the case of a plaintiff who had been directly involved in the accident rather than a mere spectator. The Court of Appeal allowed the appeal on the grounds, inter alia, that in claims for damages due to nervous shock it was in all cases incumbent on the plaintiff to prove that injury by nervous shock was reasonably foreseeable by the defendant. The plaintiff appealed to the House of Lords.


Held – (Lord Keith and Lord Jauncey dissenting) Applying the principle that the defendant had to take his victim as he found him, a negligent driver sued for damages arising out of a motor vehicle accident caused by him was liable for damages for nervous shock suffered by a primary victim of the accident if personal injury of some kind to that person was reasonably foreseeable as a result of the accident. In the case of primary victims of an accident the test in all cases was the same, namely whether the defendant could reasonably foresee that his conduct would expose the plaintiff to the risk of personal injury, whether physical or psychiatric. In the case of an affirmative answer, the duty of care was established, even though physical injury did not in fact occur. The plaintiff was not required to prove that injury by nervous shock was reasonably foreseeable by the defendant and it was irrelevant that the defendant could not have foreseen that the plaintiff had an 'eggshell personality' since (per Lord Browne-Wilkinson) it was established by medical science that psychiatric illness could be suffered as a consequence of an accident although not demonstrably attributable directly to physical injury to the plaintiff. It followed that the appeal would be allowed
